question.text: In a Value Averaging Investment Plan, compared to a regular SIP, the investor:

answer.text: Invests the same fixed amount every period regardless of market conditions
Invests more when the market is down and less when the market is up
Stops investing when the market is at all-time highs

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Value averaging adjusts the investment amount based on a target growth rate. If actual portfolio value falls below the target, more money is invested to make up the shortfall. If it exceeds the target, less is invested. This naturally leads to buying more in falling markets and less in rising markets. **The Trap:** Regular SIP invests a FIXED amount regardless of market direction — value averaging VARIES the amount based on portfolio performance. **Cross-Exam Context:** Value averaging vs SIP is tested in V-A and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Value averaging is theoretically superior to SIP but practically complex — most clients are better served by simple SIPs due to the automation advantage. Only recommend value averaging to disciplined, financially savvy clients.
